Name: adaptive_layer_skipping
Title: Adaptive Layer Skipping for Latency Optimization in Transformer Models
Short Hypothesis: By dynamically adapting layer skipping based on real-time profiling of latency and throughput, we can achieve significant performance improvements in transformer model inference without compromising accuracy.
Related Work: Existing work has explored layer skipping and model distillation, but these approaches often rely on static heuristics or pre-defined rules. Our proposal distinguishes itself by employing runtime profiling to inform layer execution decisions, which allows for more responsive and context-aware optimizations that adapt to varying input complexities and hardware conditions.
Abstract: Transformer models have revolutionized natural language processing but often suffer from high inference latency due to their deep architectures. We propose a novel approach called Adaptive Layer Skipping (ALS), which leverages real-time profiling of kernel performance metrics to determine which layers to skip during inference dynamically. By continuously monitoring latency, throughput, and occupancy metrics for each layer invocation, ALS can intelligently decide to execute only the most relevant layers based on the input characteristics and current hardware conditions. Our approach is grounded in empirical evaluations on commodity GPUs, and we will report detailed profiling results that demonstrate the effectiveness of ALS in optimizing latency without sacrificing model accuracy. We expect to achieve significant reductions in wall-clock latency while maintaining or improving tokens-per-second throughput. The experimental results will provide insights into the performance trade-offs and hardware interactions that make this approach viable for real-world deployment.
Experiments: 1. Implementation of a baseline transformer model with standard layer execution. 2. Implementation of the Adaptive Layer Skipping mechanism with real-time profiling and dynamic decision-making. 3. Conduct A/B tests comparing the latencies, throughput, and accuracy of the baseline and ALS models under various workloads. 4. Analyze the profiling metrics including occupancy, memory bandwidth, and warp stall reasons to attribute performance gains to specific optimizations. Metrics to evaluate include p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. The overhead of real-time profiling may offset some performance gains, particularly under low-latency requirements. 2. The effectiveness of layer skipping may vary with different model architectures and input distributions, necessitating careful calibration. 3. Potential degradation in accuracy if the decision-making process is not finely tuned.
